explanation: Combine over 15: \(\dfrac{3k^5 + 5k^3 + 7k}{15}\). Factorise the numerator: \(3k^5+5k^3+7k = k(3k^4+5k^2+7)\). Now rewrite as \(\dfrac{k^5-k}{5} + \dfrac{k^3-k}{3} + k\). Since \(k^5-k = k(k-1)(k+1)(k^2+1)\) is always divisible by 5 (product of 5 consecutive-style factors), and \(k^3-k = k(k-1)(k+1)\) is always divisible by 3 (product of 3 consecutive integers), and \(k\) is a natural number, the entire expression is a natural number.
name: Divisibility of Algebraic Expression

text: Let \(P(n): 2 + 2^2 + 2^3 + \cdots + 2^n = 2^{n+1}\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). Then which of the following is true?
explanation: The statement \(P(n)\) as written is \(2+2^2+\cdots+2^n = 2^{n+1}\), which is actually incorrect (the correct sum is \(2^{n+1}-2\)). So \(P(n)\) is NOT true for all \(n\). However, the inductive step — if \(P(m)\) is true, we can show \(P(m+1)\) follows — holds structurally. Assuming \(P(m)\) is true, \(P(m+1) = P(m)+2^{m+1} = 2^{m+1}+2^{m+1} = 2^{m+2}\), which matches the form of \(P(m+1)\). So the implication \(P(m) \Rightarrow P(m+1)\) is valid.
name: Principle of Mathematical Induction — Implication Step

text: \(a^n + b^n\) is divisible by __________ if \(n\) is any odd positive integer.
explanation: For any odd positive integer \(n\), the factorisation \(a^n + b^n = (a+b)(a^{n-1} - a^{n-2}b + a^{n-3}b^2 - \cdots + b^{n-1})\) holds. Therefore \(a^n+b^n\) is always divisible by \((a+b)\) when \(n\) is odd.
name: Divisibility of aⁿ + bⁿ

text: Let \(P(n): 1^2+2^2+3^2+\cdots+n^2 = \dfrac{6(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)+2n^3+3n^2+n}{6}\) for all \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). Then which of the following is correct?
explanation: We know \(1^2+2^2+\cdots+n^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\). The given RHS \(= (n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)+\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\). For the two sides to be equal, the product \((n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)\) must equal zero, which happens when \(n \in \{1,2,3,\ldots,2020\}\). So \(P(n)\) is true for all \(n \leq 2020\).
name: Validity Range of an Induction Statement

text: The sum of the cubes of three consecutive natural numbers is always divisible by
explanation: Let the three consecutive natural numbers be \(n-1, n, n+1\) (where \(n \geq 2\)). Their sum of cubes: \((n-1)^3+n^3+(n+1)^3 = n^3-3n^2+3n-1+n^3+n^3+3n^2+3n+1 = 3n^3+6n = 3n(n^2+2)\). Now \(n^2+2 \equiv 0+2,\ 1+2,\ 4+2 \pmod{3} \equiv 2,\ 0,\ 0 \pmod 3\)... checking: when \(n\) is a multiple of 3, \(3n\) gives factor of 9; when \(n\) is not a multiple of 3, \(n^2+2 \equiv 0\pmod3\). So \(3n(n^2+2)\) is always divisible by 9.
name: Divisibility of Sum of Cubes of Consecutive Numbers

explanation: For \(n \geq 10\), \(n!\) ends in at least two zeros, so it contributes 0 to the tens digit. Thus we only need the tens digit of \(1!+4!+7! = 1+24+5040 = 5065\). The tens digit is 6. Now \(3! = 6\). So the tens digit is \(3! = 6\).
name: Tens Digit of Sum of Factorials

text: For which value of \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) does \(n!\) have exactly 13 trailing zeros?
explanation: The number of trailing zeros in \(n!\) equals the exponent of 5 in \(n!\), given by \(\left\lfloor\dfrac{n}{5}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\dfrac{n}{25}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\dfrac{n}{125}\right\rfloor+\cdots\). For \(n=57\): \(\lfloor57/5\rfloor+\lfloor57/25\rfloor = 11+2 = 13\). For \(n=54\): \(10+2=12\). For \(n=59\): \(11+2=13\). So \(n=57\) is the first value with exactly 13 trailing zeros.
name: Trailing Zeros in Factorial

options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
explanation: The sum \(1+(1+2+4)+(4+6+9)+\cdots+(81+90+100)\) can be written as \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{10}\left[(r-1)^2+(r-1)r+r^2\right] = \sum_{r=1}^{10}\left[r^3-(r-1)^3\right]\) (since \(a^3-b^3=(a-b)(a^2+ab+b^2)\)). This telescopes to \(10^3-0^3=1000\). So (R) directly explains (A) — both are true and (R) is the correct explanation.
name: Assertion-Reason: Telescoping Sum

text: The sum of first \(n\) terms of the series \(\dfrac{3}{5}+\dfrac{21}{25}+\dfrac{117}{125}+\cdots\) is
explanation: Each term: \(\dfrac{3}{5}=1-\dfrac{2}{5}\), \(\dfrac{21}{25}=1-\dfrac{4}{25}\), \(\dfrac{117}{125}=1-\dfrac{8}{125}\), so the general term is \(1-\left(\dfrac{2}{5}\right)^k\). Sum of \(n\) terms \(= n - \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(\dfrac{2}{5}\right)^k = n - \dfrac{\frac{2}{5}\left(1-\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^n\right)}{1-\frac{2}{5}} = n - \dfrac{2}{3}\left(1-\left(\dfrac{2}{5}\right)^n\right) = n - \dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}\cdot\dfrac{2^n}{5^n} = n + \dfrac{2^{n+1}}{3\times5^n} - \dfrac{2}{3}\).
name: Sum of Series using GP

text: If \(15^k\) divides \(47!\) but \(15^{k+1}\) does not divide it, then \(k =\)
explanation: Since \(15=3\times5\), the exponent of 15 in \(47!\) equals the exponent of 5 in \(47!\) (the limiting prime). Exponent of 5 in \(47!\) \(= \left\lfloor\dfrac{47}{5}\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\dfrac{47}{25}\right\rfloor = 9+1=10\). So \(k=10\).
name: Highest Power of 15 in 47!

explanation: The sum equals \(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{i(i+1)(2i+1)}{6}\). Expanding and summing: \(\dfrac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(2i^3+3i^2+i) = \dfrac{1}{6}\left[2\cdot\dfrac{n^2(n+1)^2}{4}+3\cdot\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}+\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right]\). After simplification, this gives \(\dfrac{n(n+1)^2(n+2)}{12}\).
name: Sum of Cumulative Squares

text: Which of the following is divisible by \(x^2-y^2\) for all \(x \neq y\)?
explanation: We need divisibility by \(x^2-y^2=(x-y)(x+y)\). \(x^n-y^n\) is divisible by \((x-y)\) for all \(n\), and by \((x+y)\) only when \(n\) is even. \(x^{2n+1}+y^{2n+1}\) is divisible by \((x+y)\) since \(2n+1\) is odd. So \((x^n-y^n)(x^{2n+1}+y^{2n+1})\) is divisible by \((x-y)(x+y)=x^2-y^2\) for all \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
name: Divisibility by x²-y²

explanation: The general term is \(\dfrac{1}{(3k-1)(3k+2)}\). Using partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{(3k-1)(3k+2)}=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{3k-1}-\dfrac{1}{3k+2}\right)\). The series telescopes: \(\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{50}\right)=\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot\dfrac{48}{100}=\dfrac{48}{300}=\dfrac{4}{25}\). (The 16th term has \(k=16\): denominator \((3\cdot16-1)(3\cdot16+2)=47\cdot50\), so last term in telescope is \(\dfrac{1}{50}\).)
name: Telescoping Series Sum

text: The statement \(n^5-5n^3+4n\) is divisible by \(120\) is true for
explanation: Factorise: \(n^5-5n^3+4n = n(n^4-5n^2+4) = n(n^2-1)(n^2-4) = n(n-1)(n+1)(n-2)(n+2) = (n-2)(n-1)n(n+1)(n+2)\). This is the product of 5 consecutive integers, which is always divisible by \(5! = 120\). So the statement holds for all positive integers \(n\).
name: Divisibility by 5! = 120

explanation: By verification at \(k=0\): \(2\cdot4^1+3^1=8+3=11=11\cdot1\), so \(t=1\). Then \(2\cdot4^3+3^4=128+81=209=11(p\cdot1+3^q)\). So \(11(p+3^q)=209 \Rightarrow p+3^q=19\). From option (a): \(p=16\) and \(q=3(0)+1=1\), giving \(16+3=19\) ✓. General step confirms \((p,q)=(16,\ 3k+1)\).
name: Inductive Step Verification

text: For any natural number \(n\), \((15\times5^{2n})+(2\times2^{3n})\) is divisible by
explanation: For \(n=1\): \(15\times25+2\times8=375+16=391=17\times23\). Divisible by 17. For \(n=2\): \(15\times625+2\times64=9375+128=9503=17\times559\). Divisible by 17. By induction: assume \(15\times5^{2k}+2\times2^{3k}=17m\). Then \(15\times5^{2(k+1)}+2\times2^{3(k+1)}=25\times15\times5^{2k}+8\times2\times2^{3k}=25(17m-2\times2^{3k})+8\times2\times2^{3k}=25\times17m-50\times2^{3k}+16\times2^{3k}=17(25m)-34\times2^{3k}=17(25m-2\times2^{3k})\). Divisible by 17.
name: Divisibility by 17 via Induction

text: If \(a_n = \underbrace{\sqrt{7+\sqrt{7+\sqrt{7+\cdots}}}}_{n\text{ times}}\), then which of the following is true?
explanation: The limiting value \(a_\infty\) satisfies \(a_\infty = \sqrt{7+a_\infty}\), so \(a_\infty^2-a_\infty-7=0 \Rightarrow a_\infty=\dfrac{1+\sqrt{29}}{2}\approx\dfrac{1+5.39}{2}\approx3.19\). Since the sequence is increasing and bounded above by \(a_\infty<4\), we have \(a_n < a_\infty < 4\) for all \(n \geq 1\). Thus \(a_n < 4,\ \forall n \geq 1\).
name: Nested Radical Sequence Bound

explanation: The general factor: \(1+\dfrac{2k+1}{k^2}=\dfrac{k^2+2k+1}{k^2}=\dfrac{(k+1)^2}{k^2}\). So the product is \(\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{(k+1)^2}{k^2}=\dfrac{(n+1)^2}{1}=(n+1)^2\). Setting \((n+1)^2=121 \Rightarrow n+1=11 \Rightarrow n=10\).
name: Telescoping Product

text: If \(2(4^{2n+1})+3^{3n+1}\) is divisible by \(k\), \(k>1\) for all \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), then the value of \(k\) is
name: Divisibility by k via Induction
